intro_text:
Te Oneroa-a-Tōhē, mapped in English as Ninety Mile Beach, runs up the west coast of the Aupōuri Peninsula at the far north of New Zealand's North Island, in the Northland region. The English name overstates it: the beach is about 88 kilometres long, roughly 55 miles, not ninety. It faces the Tasman Sea, ends near Cape Reinga / Te Rerenga Wairua to the north, and meets the small settlement of Ahipara at its southern end. Te Oneroa-a-Tōhē means "the long beach of Tōhē," named for the Te Aupōuri ancestor Tōhē, who is said to have travelled its length.

The defining fact about the beach is legal, not scenic. It is a gazetted public road under New Zealand law, a highway that vehicles may drive along the firm wet sand at low tide, running parallel to State Highway 1 inland. Because it is a road, ordinary road rules apply and only road-licensed vehicles are permitted on it. The hazard sits inside that permission. The Tasman tide range here exceeds three metres and floods fast, soft dry sand swallows tyres, and the Te Paki Stream crossing at the northern end runs across quicksand. Rental companies bar their cars from the beach and most rental agreements void all cover for driving on it, yet visitors attempt it anyway and vehicles are stranded and lost to the incoming sea most years.

The northern end carries the deepest meaning. Te Oneroa-a-Tōhē is the final stretch of Te Ara Wairua, the spirits' pathway, along which Māori believe the spirits of the dead travel north to Te Rerenga Wairua at Cape Reinga. There they descend by the roots of a wind-bent pōhutukawa, estimated at around 800 years old, into the sea on the journey back to the ancestral homeland of Hawaiki. Behind the beach rise the Te Paki sand dunes, some reaching about 150 metres, now used for sandboarding.

The beach is governed in partnership. The 2015 Te Hiku Treaty settlement created the Te Oneroa-a-Tōhē Beach Board, a co-governance body of the four Te Hiku iwi, Te Aupōuri, Te Rarawa, NgāiTakoto and Ngāti Kuri, together with the Far North District and Northland Regional councils, and confirmed the dual name. Its shellfish carry their own controls: the toheroa fishery has been closed nationally since 1982, and taking toheroa here can draw fines up to NZ$20,000, though customary permits exist for iwi.
honest_reckoning_note:
The danger on Te Oneroa-a-Tōhē is a product of its own status. Because the beach is a legally gazetted road, it reads on a map like any other route and invites drivers who would never take a car onto open sand otherwise. The reality the map omits is that the road floods. The Tasman tidal range exceeds three metres and rises quickly, the dry upper sand bogs two-wheel-drive cars, and the Te Paki Stream that vehicles cross to reach the beach near the northern end runs over quicksand that has swallowed cars whole.

Rental firms understand this and forbid the beach in their contracts, voiding insurance for anyone who drives it. That clause is not bureaucratic caution. Every year vehicles are bogged on the rising tide, abandoned, and lost to the sea or buried in the Te Paki sands. The standing local advice is blunt: if water reaches the car and the tide is coming in, leave the vehicle and walk to safety. The beach is genuinely drivable, but only by buses and 4WDs run by operators who read the tide tables, which is why most visitors who want the length of it go by guided coach rather than their own hire car.

day_in_time.dawn: At low water near dawn the wet sand sets hard enough to drive, and the licensed tour coaches time their northbound runs to this window. The Tasman is grey-blue under a flat horizon, the dunes still in shadow, and the beach effectively empty before the day's traffic and tide arrive.
day_in_time.midday: By the middle of the day the tour buses are working the firm sand, sandboarders climb the Te Paki dunes inland, and anglers fish the gutters for snapper. The tide governs everything: as it turns and floods, the drivable margin narrows toward the soft upper sand.
day_in_time.golden: Late afternoon light rakes low across 88 kilometres of open sand and a west-facing coast that takes the sun straight down into the Tasman. The last operators clear the beach ahead of the rising tide; this is the safe window closing, not opening.
day_in_time.night: After dark the beach is unlit and untravelled, with no facilities along its length and the nearest services back at Ahipara or Kaitaia. The northern end runs toward Cape Reinga, where Māori tradition places the departure of spirits out to sea.

food_drink.name: Nothing on the sand
food_drink.description: There are no shops, kiosks, or fresh water anywhere along the 88-kilometre beach. Carry your own food, water, and a full fuel tank; buy supplies before you leave Kaitaia or Ahipara, the nearest towns with services.
food_drink.where: Plan around the beach, not on it

food_drink.name: Snapper from the surf
food_drink.description: The beach is a surfcasting fishery for snapper, the catch behind the annual NgāiTakoto Ninety Mile Beach Snapper Bonanza competition. A valid New Zealand recreational fishing approach applies; this is line-fishing the gutters, not a sold meal.
food_drink.where: The open surf along the beach

food_drink.name: Toheroa and tuatua (with a warning)
food_drink.description: The beach's shellfish are toheroa and the more common tuatua. Toheroa harvesting has been banned nationally since 1982; taking them here risks fines up to NZ$20,000. Tuatua are not under the same ban. Customary gathering by iwi is provided for under permit.
food_drink.where: The intertidal sand, under strict legal controls

timeline.title: The legend of Te Houtaewa
timeline.description: Te Houtaewa of Te Aupōuri, a famed runner and descendant of the chief Te Ikanui, raids a Te Rarawa village at Ahipara, steals baskets of kūmara, and outruns his pursuers the length of Te Oneroa-a-Tōhē back to Te Kao. The feat is still commemorated by his descendants in a beach race.
timeline.source: Te Ara Encyclopedia of New Zealand: Muriwhenua tribes

timeline.title: National toheroa ban
timeline.description: After a collapse in numbers, the toheroa fishery is closed across New Zealand. Despite a partial recovery on Ninety Mile Beach the ban remains, with customary harvest by iwi the only exception.
timeline.source: NZ Herald: Toheroa off limits at Ninety Mile Beach; Stuff

timeline.title: Te Hiku settlement and the Beach Board
timeline.description: The Te Hiku Claims Settlement legislation passes its third reading, establishing the Te Oneroa-a-Tōhē co-governance Beach Board of the four Te Hiku iwi with the Far North District and Northland Regional councils, and confirming the dual name Te Oneroa-a-Tōhē / Ninety Mile Beach.

zones.name: Ahipara (southern end)
zones.character: The small settlement at the beach's southern end, the usual access point and the nearest community with cafés, a store, and basic services. The Te Houtaewa legend places the stolen kūmara raid at a Te Rarawa village here.
zones.best_for: Access, supplies, and the southern launch onto the sand
zones.notes: Around 46 km from the beach centroid; the practical base for southern access.

zones.name: The drivable sand highway
zones.character: The firm wet intertidal sand that is gazetted as a public road, drivable at low tide by road-licensed vehicles running parallel to State Highway 1 inland. The tide and soft upper sand make the window narrow and the hazard real.
zones.best_for: The beach-as-highway drive, with a guide
zones.notes: Rental cars are barred and uninsured here; tides flood fast and strand vehicles.

zones.name: Te Paki dunes and stream
zones.character: Giant sand dunes behind the northern beach, some rising to roughly 150 metres, used for sandboarding. The Te Paki Stream is the northern vehicle access route onto the beach and crosses quicksand.
zones.notes: The Te Paki Stream crossing runs over quicksand that has swallowed vehicles.

zones.name: Toward Cape Reinga / Te Rerenga Wairua
zones.character: The northern terminus of the beach runs toward Cape Reinga, Te Rerenga Wairua, the leaping-off place of spirits in Māori tradition, marked by an ancient pōhutukawa and a lighthouse where the Tasman meets the Pacific.
zones.best_for: The spiritual and geographic end of the land
zones.notes: Cape Reinga is reached by road, not by driving to the very end of the sand.

landmarks.name: Te Rerenga Wairua pōhutukawa (Cape Reinga spirit tree)
landmarks.description: An ancient wind-bent pōhutukawa on the headland at Cape Reinga, estimated at around 800 years old. In Māori tradition the spirits of the dead descend its exposed roots into the sea to begin the journey back to Hawaiki. The beach is the final stretch of Te Ara Wairua, the spirits' pathway, leading to it.

landmarks.name: Te Paki Sand Dunes
landmarks.description: Desert-like sand dunes behind the northern end of the beach, some rising to roughly 150 metres. They abut Ninety Mile Beach and are the main sandboarding site in the Far North, usually visited as part of Cape Reinga tours.

landmarks.name: Cape Reinga Lighthouse
landmarks.description: The lighthouse at Cape Reinga, near the northern terminus of the beach, where the Tasman Sea meets the Pacific Ocean. It stands beside the headland that Māori tradition marks as Te Rerenga Wairua, the departing place of spirits.

cultural_refs.title: Te Houtaewa and the stolen kūmara
cultural_refs.description: The traditional account of Te Houtaewa of Te Aupōuri, a famed athlete who raided a Te Rarawa village at Ahipara, stole baskets of kūmara, and outran his pursuers the full length of Te Oneroa-a-Tōhē to Te Kao. The story anchors the beach as a place of running and endurance.

cultural_refs.title: Te Ara Wairua — the spirits' pathway
cultural_refs.description: In Māori belief the beach is the final stretch of Te Ara Wairua, along which the spirits of the dead travel north to Te Rerenga Wairua at Cape Reinga, descending the roots of an ancient pōhutukawa into the sea toward Hawaiki. This makes the beach a spiritual route, not only a road.

cultural_refs.title: Te Houtaewa Beach Race
cultural_refs.description: An annual race run by the descendants of Te Houtaewa on Te Oneroa-a-Tōhē, commemorating his legendary run and billed as a marathon-distance event run entirely on beach terrain. It draws runners from across New Zealand and overseas.

recurring_events.name: Te Houtaewa Beach Race
recurring_events.description: A long-running commemorative race organised by Te Houtaewa's descendants along Te Oneroa-a-Tōhē, run entirely on the beach and recalling his legendary kūmara-laden run from Ahipara to Te Kao.

recurring_events.name: NgāiTakoto Ninety Mile Beach Snapper Bonanza
recurring_events.description: A five-day surfcasting competition on the beach that draws anglers from across New Zealand chasing the largest snapper. It is run in association with NgāiTakoto.

things_to_know.label: It is a legal road, with legal-road hazards
things_to_know.note: The beach is a gazetted highway; only road-licensed vehicles may drive it, at low tide. The Tasman tide floods fast and strands cars, so check tide tables and stay off the soft upper sand.

things_to_know.label: Rental cars are barred
things_to_know.note: Rental companies forbid the beach and most agreements void all insurance for driving on it. Vehicles are lost to the tide and the Te Paki quicksand every year; take a guided coach or experienced 4WD.

things_to_know.label: Toheroa are protected
things_to_know.note: Taking toheroa shellfish is banned nationally since 1982; fines reach NZ$20,000. Tuatua are not under the same ban. Customary harvest by iwi is permitted.

things_to_know.label: Respect the spiritual ground
things_to_know.note: The beach is part of Te Ara Wairua, the spirits' pathway to Cape Reinga / Te Rerenga Wairua, and is co-governed under the 2015 Te Hiku settlement. Treat the northern end and Cape Reinga as sacred.

access_note: Access is from Ahipara at the south or via the Te Paki Stream at the north; Kaitaia (about 42 km, Kaitaia Airport KAT) is the nearest town with full services. The drivable sand is gazetted as a road but is safe only around low tide and only for road-licensed, sand-capable vehicles. Rental cars are prohibited and uninsured on the beach; most visitors drive its length by guided coach.
spike_explainer:
Te Oneroa-a-Tōhē is a beach with a contradiction written into its legal status: it is a road. Under New Zealand law the firm wet sand of Ninety Mile Beach is a gazetted public highway, drivable at low tide by road-licensed vehicles, running roughly parallel to State Highway 1 a few kilometres inland. That single fact, more than its length or its light, is what defines the place and what makes it dangerous.

**The length the name gets wrong.** The English name promises ninety miles. The beach delivers about 88 kilometres, roughly 55 miles. The discrepancy is old and much-retold, variously blamed on missionaries or drovers misjudging distance by the time it took bullock teams or horses to walk it on soft sand. Whatever its origin, the name stuck while the measurement did not, and "Ninety Mile Beach" survives as a place-name that is wrong by a third.

**Why a beach is a road.** Te Oneroa-a-Tōhē has been used as a travel route for as long as people have lived in the Far North. At low tide the intertidal sand packs hard enough to carry vehicles, and for the long, narrow Aupōuri Peninsula it offered a flat, straight surface that the dune country behind could not. New Zealand formalised that use: the beach is gazetted as a legal road, which means ordinary road rules apply and only road-licensed vehicles may travel it. On a map it reads like any other route north.

**Where the road turns lethal.** The map omits the tide. The Tasman tidal range here exceeds three metres and floods quickly across a low, flat beach, so the drivable margin can vanish in the time it takes to stop and take a photograph. The dry upper sand bogs two-wheel-drive cars. And at the northern end, the access point onto the beach is the Te Paki Stream, which runs over quicksand that has swallowed entire vehicles. The result is a road that drowns. Every year cars are bogged on the incoming tide, abandoned, and lost to the sea or buried in the Te Paki sands. The standing advice from locals and operators is unsentimental: if water is reaching the car and the tide is rising, leave it and walk to safety.

**Why rental cars are banned.** Because the hazard is real and recurrent, New Zealand rental companies forbid their vehicles on the beach, and most rental agreements void all insurance cover for driving on it. This is not fine-print caution; it is a direct response to the rate at which hire cars are written off here. The beach is genuinely drivable, but by buses and 4WDs operated by people who read the tide charts, which is why the standard way to travel its length is a guided coach rather than a self-drive hire car.

**The other road, running the other way.** Layered over the legal highway is an older route that runs in the opposite direction and is invisible on any map. In Māori tradition the beach is the final stretch of Te Ara Wairua, the spirits' pathway, along which the spirits of the dead travel north to Te Rerenga Wairua at Cape Reinga. There a wind-bent pōhutukawa, estimated at around 800 years old, marks the leaping-off place: spirits descend its roots into the sea to begin the journey back to Hawaiki. So the same 88 kilometres carries cars south-to-north at low tide and, in belief, carries the dead the same way toward the edge of the land. The gazetted road and the spirit-path share the sand.

**Who holds the beach now.** Since the 2015 Te Hiku Treaty settlement the beach has been co-governed by the Te Oneroa-a-Tōhē Beach Board, made up of the four Te Hiku iwi, Te Aupōuri, Te Rarawa, NgāiTakoto and Ngāti Kuri, alongside the Far North District and Northland Regional councils, and the settlement confirmed the dual name. The beach that the law treats as a road is, to the iwi who hold it, a taonga and an ancestor's route. Both readings are official. Both are true at once.

key_facts.label: Actual length
key_facts.source: Wikipedia: Ninety Mile Beach, New Zealand

key_facts.label: Te Paki dunes
key_facts.source: Wikipedia: Te Paki Sand Dunes; DOC

key_facts.label: Co-governance
key_facts.value: Te Oneroa-a-Tōhē Beach Board, 2015 Te Hiku settlement

margin_notes.text: The beach is a legal road, so road-licensed vehicles may drive it, but the Tasman tide range tops three metres and floods fast. Rental firms bar the sand and void insurance for it; cars are stranded and lost to the incoming tide most years.

Te Oneroa-a-Tōhē means "the long beach of Tōhē," for the Te Aupōuri ancestor Tōhē said to have travelled its length. The 2015 Te Hiku settlement confirmed the dual name and created the co-governance Beach Board of the four Te Hiku iwi.

The Te Houtaewa legend casts the beach as a running ground: a Te Aupōuri athlete who stole kūmara from a Te Rarawa village at Ahipara and outran his pursuers the whole length to Te Kao. His descendants still race the beach in his memory.

Behind the northern beach the Te Paki dunes rise to about 150 metres and now serve as a sandboarding run. The Te Paki Stream, the northern vehicle entrance to the beach, crosses quicksand that has swallowed whole cars.

The beach's toheroa shellfish have been closed to harvest nationwide since 1982 after a population collapse; taking them risks fines up to NZ$20,000. The more common tuatua are not under the same ban. Iwi may gather under customary permit.

In Māori belief the beach is the last stretch of Te Ara Wairua, the spirits' pathway. The dead travel north along it to Te Rerenga Wairua at Cape Reinga and descend an ~800-year-old pōhutukawa into the sea toward Hawaiki.

reckoning_pullquote: The map reads it as a road; the Tasman tide reads it as the sea, and strands the cars that believe the map.
